items.title: [State Sync] Account for validator-PFN connections.
items.body:
## Description
This PR updates the aptos data client in state sync to better account for validator-PFN connections. Thankfully, nothing needs to change in the data client (aside from documentation and a new config flag we've added to help gate the feature). This config flag will be used across all components.

## Testing Plan
New test infrastructure.


<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Risk**
> Adds a new `BaseConfig` field that changes the serialized config schema (though it defaults safely), and expands state-sync tests; no peer-selection logic is modified.
> 
> **Overview**
> Introduces a new `BaseConfig.enable_validator_pfn_connections` flag (default `false`) to gate/describe direct validator↔PFN connectivity.
> 
> Updates state-sync data client coverage to explicitly validate validator peer selection behavior when only PFN peers are available and when VFNs appear/disconnect, and tightens prioritization documentation/comments in `priority.rs` without changing logic.
>

items.title: Add private view functions to the ABI
items.body:
## Description
Fixes PI-156.

Private `#[view]` functions are callable via the REST API but were previously omitted from the node's ABI responses, preventing their discovery by tools like explorers. This PR updates the ABI generation logic to include private `#[view]` functions, ensuring they are properly exposed with their `private` visibility and `is_view: true` attribute.

This should hopefully not cause any issues with downstream tools (eg Surf) because they should be making all inference decisions based on privacy, is_entry, and is_view already.

## How Has This Been Tested?
Existing ABI tests were augmented to include a new private `#[view]` function alongside an existing public one. The tests now verify that both public and private `#[view]` functions are correctly included in the ABI with `is_view: true` and their respective `public` or `private` visibility. All `aptos-api` tests pass.

See the output for [this contract](https://github.com/banool/move-examples/tree/e951393b27ca15ccd628de1ae71ec9d7b7d2122f/view_function/sources/my_module.move) on localnet:


```json
items.title: [Network] Replace the network health checker with the peer monitoring service.
items.body:
## Description
This PR replaces the disconnect logic in the network health checker with the peer monitoring service, i.e., the peer monitoring service now disconnects from peers that fail to respond to ping messages. This is something that was previously done by the network health checker.

Note: The goal is to eventually remove the network health checker, as the peer monitoring service is already doing ping message verification. So, health check messages are now unnecessary.

## How Has This Been Tested?
New and existing unit tests, as well as manual verification.


<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes peer liveness failure handling and introduces new disconnect paths/config flags; misconfiguration or threshold changes could cause unexpected peer churn or reduced health enforcement.
> 
> **Overview**
> Shifts responsibility for disconnecting unresponsive peers from the network `HealthChecker` to the peer monitoring service’s latency ping logic.
> 
> Adds config knobs for both systems: `NetworkConfig.disconnect_on_health_check_failure` (default *off*) gates health-checker disconnects, while `LatencyMonitoringConfig.disconnect_from_peers_on_failures` (default *on*) plus shorter default ping interval enables peer-monitor-driven disconnects after repeated failures.
> 
> Extends networking with a new `DisconnectReason::PeerMonitoringPingFailure`, exposes a disconnect API on `PeerMonitoringServiceClient`, and updates peer-state construction and tests (including new mock-based async tests) to validate disconnect behavior.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit ce491dfc78a3e03478a83ce9af21bb790f50b4b8. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: [storage] Report RocksDB ticker statistics via Prometheus
items.body:
Add periodic reporting of per-DB RocksDB ticker counters in
`RocksdbPropertyReporter`. Unlike properties (which are per-column-family),
tickers are per-DB-instance cumulative counters.

To support querying them, `DB::open*` now takes `Options` by value instead of
by reference, and retains the `Options` in the `DB` struct. The retained
`Options` shares the same underlying `Statistics` object as the live DB, so
`Options::get_ticker_count()` returns live values. A new `DB::get_ticker_count`
method is exposed for callers. A `DB::name()` accessor is also added so the
reporter can label metrics by DB name.

Add `LedgerDb::metadata_db_raw()` for consistency with the other sub-DB
accessors (`event_db_raw`, `write_set_db_raw`, etc.), and use it in the
reporter instead of `metadata_db_arc()` to avoid unnecessary `Arc` refcount
bumps.

**Tickers reported** (metric: `aptos_rocksdb_tickers{db_name, ticker_name}`):
- `stall_micros` — total write stall time
- `compact_read_bytes` / `compact_write_bytes` — compaction I/O
- `flush_write_bytes` — memtable flush I/O
- `bloom_filter_useful`, `bloom_filter_full_positive`,
  `bloom_filter_full_true_positive` — full-key bloom filter stats
- `bloom_filter_prefix_checked`, `bloom_filter_prefix_useful`,
  `bloom_filter_prefix_true_positive` — prefix bloom filter stats
- `block_cache_hit` / `block_cache_miss` — overall block cache
- `block_cache_data_hit` / `block_cache_data_miss` — data block cache
- `block_cache_filter_hit` / `block_cache_filter_miss` — filter block cache

A single gauge is used for all DBs (no separate shard gauge) because the
`db_name` label already encodes shard info (e.g. `"state_kv_db_shard_0"`).

Requires `stats_level` to be set in the RocksDB config for non-zero values.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> **Medium Risk**
> Touches the core `aptos_schemadb::DB` open APIs and threads `Options` ownership through many storage components, so mistakes could affect DB initialization or metrics correctness. Runtime be

items.title: [storage] Replace pruner worker polling with channel-based wake
items.body:
Replace the 1ms sleep-poll loop in `PrunerWorker` with a
`sync_channel(1)`-based blocking wait.

**Before:** Each pruner worker thread (state_merkle, ledger, state_kv) woke up
every 1ms to check `is_pruning_pending()`, causing ~1000 context switches/sec
per thread even when fully idle.

**After:** The worker blocks on `recv()` when there's no pending work, and is
woken only when:
- `set_target_db_version` is called with a new target (`try_send(())`)
- The `PrunerWorker` is dropped (sender dropped → `recv()` returns `Err`)

This also eliminates the `PrunerWorkerInner` struct — the channel naturally
separates the producer (sender on `PrunerWorker`) from the consumer (receiver
moved into the worker thread). Error backoff is bumped from 1ms to 100ms.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes the pruner worker’s concurrency model from tight sleep-polling to channel-based blocking wakeups, which can affect shutdown/wakeup behavior and risk hangs if signaling is missed. Scope is limited to `PrunerWorker`, but it runs continuously in production so regressions would be noisy.
> 
> **Overview**
> Replaces `PrunerWorker`’s 1ms sleep/poll loop with a `sync_channel(1)` wake mechanism so idle pruner threads block on `recv()` and only run when signaled.
> 
> `set_target_db_version()` now `try_send(())`s to wake the worker after advancing the target version, and `Drop` signals shutdown by dropping the sender (worker exits when the channel disconnects). The old `PrunerWorkerInner` + `AtomicBool` stop flag is removed, and pruning error backoff is increased to 100ms.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit e4a6b8e6df9d590914111648df0694eee02c05d3. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: [vm] Fixing issues with bounds and recursion
items.body:
Fixes issues with bounds checking and recursion in the VM.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Medium risk because it changes production bytecode verification limits (struct/function/script bounds) behind a timed feature flag, which can cause previously-valid modules/scripts to be rejected once activated. Also tweaks instantiation-loop checking output ordering, which may affect test expectations and debugging but not execution semantics.
> 
> **Overview**
> **Adds a new timed feature to enable stricter production verifier bounds.** `aptos_prod_verifier_config` now takes `TimedFeatures` and, when `EnableStrictBoundsInProdConfig` is enabled, enforces new caps for struct definitions/variants/fields, function definitions, and script basic blocks.
> 
> Updates callers (VM config wiring, backup restore module validation, and Move fuzz targets) to pass timed features (often via `TimedFeaturesBuilder::enable_all()`), and adjusts an enum-variants e2e test fixture to align with the new 64-variant cap.
> 
> Improves determinism in the Move bytecode verifier’s instantiation-loop checker by switching SCC algorithm to `kosaraju_scc` and sorting reported nodes/edges, updating the associated compiler v2 golden output; also fixes a missing newline in a Move prover doc.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit acb3cc3a02edecb8e1bde6e60d606741a687a040. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: [cp][aptos-release-v1.41] [execution] Fix cache flush
items.body:
# Backport

This will backport the following commits from `main` to `aptos-release-v1.41`:
 - [[execution] Fix cache flush (#18884)](https://github.com/aptos-labs/aptos-core/pull/18884)

<!--- Backport version: 9.5.1 -->

### Questions ?
Please refer to the [Backport tool documentation](https://github.com/sorenlouv/backport)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Adjusts cache-flushing behavior in the block executor and Move runtime environment; mistakes could cause unnecessary cache churn or, worse, stale/inconsistent cached data across transaction slices.
> 
> **Overview**
> **Fixes cache invalidation/flush behavior** in the block executor by renaming `GlobalModuleCache::flush` to `flush_all_caches` and updating call sites to clear modules *and* derived layout state together when resetting between slices, on environment changes, on parallel fallback, or when the module cache exceeds size limits.
> 
> **Unifies flushing of runtime intern caches** in `ModuleCacheManager::check_ready`: instead of selectively flushing individual intern pools, it now flushes the Move `RuntimeEnvironment` caches and the global module cache together when any interned cache (module IDs, struct names, types, or type vectors) exceeds configured thresholds, and keeps layout flushing as a separate, layout-only path. Metrics are updated to `GLOBAL_LAYOUT_CACHE_NUM_ENTRIES`, and new tests assert that each threshold breach fully clears the relevant caches.
> 
> **Exposes small Move VM cache introspection** by making `RuntimeEnvironment::ty_tag_cache()` and `TypeTagCache` public and adding `TypeTagCache::len()` to support the new cache-flush tests.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 530ac0d7930337d619e970d4cfaeacc95ffe088d. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: Rosetta fee payer tracking (#18799)
items.body:
* [rosetta] Fix fee payer tracking: use fee_payer_address instead of sender for fee operations

When a transaction has a fee payer (via FeePayer authenticator), the gas fee and storage fee refund operations should attribute the fee to the fee payer address, not the transaction sender. Previously, txn.sender() was always used, which incorrectly showed the sender as the fee payer in the Rosetta response.

Now we check for a fee_payer_address in the transaction authenticator and fall back to sender() only when no fee payer is present.



* [rosetta] Add tests for fee payer tracking in Rosetta operations

Add two tests that verify fee operations are attributed to the fee payer address (not the sender) when a FeePayer authenticator is present:

- test_fee_payer_transfer_attributes_fee_to_fee_payer: verifies a transfer with a fee payer shows the fee on the fee payer's account
- test_fee_payer_mint_attributes_fee_to_fee_payer: verifies a mint with a fee payer shows the fee on the fee payer's account

Also adds a test_fee_payer_transaction helper that constructs a TransactionOnChainData with a FeePayer authenticator.



* [rosetta] Deduplicate gas payer resolution into a single computation

Extract the fee_payer_address().unwrap_or_else(sender) logic into a single gas_payer variable computed once per user transaction, then reuse it for both the storage fee refund and the gas fee operation. This eliminates duplication and prevents the two code paths from drifting apart.



* [rosetta] Add storage fee refund attribution tests

Add two tests covering the storage fee refund path with FeeStatement events that have a non-zero storage_fee_refund:

- test_fee_payer_storage_refund_attributes_to_fee_payer: verifies that the storage refund deposit AND gas fee are both attributed to the fee payer
- test_no_fee_payer_storage_refund_attributes_to_sender: verifies that without a fee payer, both fall back to the sender

This ensures both changed code paths (

items.title: [Network] Remove subscription logic from PeersAndMetadata.
items.body:
## Description
This PR removes the subscription logic from `PeersAndMetadata` (this is a legacy feature that introduced more tech-debt than necessary). More specifically, it: (i) removes the `subscribe()` and `broadcast()` methods; (ii) removes the dependency on the `NewPeer` and `LostPeer` events in the network health checker; (iii) cleans up a bunch of code related to this feature; and (iv) adds new and missing unit tests.

Note: in the future, the network health checker will be completely removed and replaced by the peer monitoring service.

## Testing Plan
New and existing test infrastructure.




<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes connection tracking semantics from event-driven to polling in the health checker and tests; risk is mainly around missed/late peer detection timing or increased polling overhead, but scoped to networking internals.
> 
> **Overview**
> Removes legacy connection subscription/broadcast support from `PeersAndMetadata` (drops `subscribe()`/`broadcast()` and associated locking/logging), shifting callers to query `get_connected_peers_and_metadata()` instead.
> 
> Updates the health checker to stop consuming `NewPeer`/`LostPeer` streams and instead periodically diffs the current connected-peer set against an internal `known_peers` cache, with expanded unit tests for new/lost peer detection.

items.title: [prover] Fix behavioral predicates on opaque functions
items.body:
## Description

Fix Boogie compilation error ("call to undeclared procedure") when behavioral predicates (`result_of<f>`, `aborts_of<f>`) are used on opaque functions.

The `$apply` procedure (which dispatches closure calls) emitted a direct `call` to the target function's Boogie procedure. But opaque functions have no Boogie procedure body generated, causing the compilation error. The fix detects opaque targets in the closure variant loop and uses behavioral predicates (`$aborts_of` evaluator + `$result_of` function) instead of a direct call, matching how function parameter variants are already handled.

## How Has This Been Tested?

- New test: `opaque_behavioral_predicates.move` covering simple opaque, recursive opaque (bounded), and mutually recursive opaque functions with behavioral predicates. All verify cleanly.
- Full prover test suite passes (173 tests, 0 failures).

## Type of Change

items.title: [Storage] Add hot state cache age metrics
items.body:
Report per-shard MRU/LRU `hot_since_version` gauges to help assess whether
the hot state cache capacity is adequate. A small version span between MRU
and LRU indicates items are being evicted too quickly (cache too small).

New metrics:
- `aptos_hot_state_shard_gauge{shard_id, name="mru_hot_since_version"}`
- `aptos_hot_state_shard_gauge{shard_id, name="lru_hot_since_version"}`
- `aptos_storage_gauge{name="hot_state_max_lru_hot_since_version"}` (aggregate)
- `aptos_storage_gauge{name="hot_state_min_lru_hot_since_version"}` (aggregate)

The aggregate metrics track the max and min LRU across shards rather than
MRU, since MRU versions are similar across shards (all near the latest
committed version). Max LRU identifies the shard under the most eviction
pressure (shallowest cache depth), which is more useful for capacity planning.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

items.title: [compiler v2] enable warnings on unused private funcs, structs, and constants
items.body:
## Description
This PR adds supports for warnings on unused private structs, unused constants, and unused private functions.
- Usage of constants, by functions and other constants, are tracked on-the-fly when building the move model (they are replaced by values during model building).
- Usage of structs, by functions and other structs, are tracked after the move model is built.
- Unused private functions are already collected.

This PR introduces new experiments `unused-constant-check`, `unused-function-check`, and `unused-struct-check` to enabled the checks (disabled by default for backward compatibility). The checks are only available with language version 2.4 or higher.

This PR also refactor `check_access_and_use`
- Separate access checks from usage checks
- Split the access check code into a before-inlining version and an after-inlining version
- Re-structure the access check code to be more readable

### Known False Positives and Mitigations (assuming compilation with `aptos move compile`):

1. Functions/structs/constants only used in test code.
    - This cannot be detected, as test code is not included for compilation
    - Mitigation: include guidance in warning message: `Consider adding `#[test_only]` if test-only ...`

2. Functions/structs/constants only used in verification code.
    - Mitigation: include guidance in warning message: `Consider adding ... `#[verify_only]` (if verify-only) ...`

3. Private functions that are only called by the VM: 
    - All such functions, known so far, belong to the `aptos-framework` package. Do not affect users directly

4. Structs that have been published but no longer used

items.title: [cli] Extract aptos move commands into standalone aptos-move-cli crate
items.body:
## Summary

Extract all `aptos move` subcommands from `crates/aptos` into a new `aptos-move/cli` crate (`aptos-move-cli`) so the Move CLI can be built and tested independently of the full Aptos CLI.

Introduces `DiagWriter`, a clonable `WriteColor` wrapper that threads compiler/tool diagnostic output through the build pipeline instead of writing directly to stderr. This eliminates fd-level stderr capture hacks in tests and enables callers to programmatically intercept all diagnostic output. `DiagWriter` lives in `move-core-types` so it can be shared across `move-package`, `aptos-framework`, and `aptos-move-cli` without introducing new dependency edges.

Closes #18821.

### Architecture

- **`aptos-move-cli`** (`aptos-move/cli/`) — contains all `aptos move` subcommands, local simulation, and a standalone `move` binary
- **`aptos-cli-common`** (`crates/aptos-cli-common/`) — shared types (`TransactionOptions`, `CliConfig`, etc.) used by both crates
- **`AptosContext` trait** — abstracts network operations (transaction submission, view functions); the full CLI registers `RealAptosContext` at startup, while the standalone binary gives clear errors for network commands
- **`MoveDebugger` trait** — decouples local simulation from `aptos-move-debugger` at the crate level, using `DynStateView` for type erasure
- **`MoveEnv`** — holds the `DiagWriter`, `AptosContext`, and `MoveDebuggerFactory`; injected into every command via `#[clap(skip)]`

### What moved where

| From | To |
|------|-----|
| `crates/aptos/src/move_tool/` | `aptos-move/cli/src/` (commands, bytecode, coverage, fmt, lint, etc.) |
| `crates/aptos/src/common/local_simulation.rs` | `aptos-move/cli/src/local_simulation.rs` |
| `crates/aptos/src/common/transactions.rs` | `aptos-move/cli/src/transactions.rs` |
| Shared types from `crates/aptos/src/common/types.rs` | `crates/aptos-cli-common/src/{options,config,types,utils}.rs` |
| Network dispatch logic | `crates/aptos/src/aptos_context.rs` (`RealAptosContext`) |

items.title: [vm] Extract aptos-framework-natives and aptos-release-bundle to break rebuild chains
items.body:
## Description

The `aptos-framework` crate depends on the entire Move compiler stack (move-compiler-v2, move-prover, move-package, etc.) because it bundles both build tooling and runtime native functions. This means any change to the compiler or prover triggers a rebuild of `aptos-framework` and everything downstream — `aptos-vm`, `aptos-cached-packages`, `vm-genesis`, consensus, API, node, and ~30 other crates.

This PR makes two crate extractions to break the rebuild chains:

### 1. `aptos-framework-natives`

Extracts the `natives` module into a standalone crate with zero compiler dependencies. `aptos-vm` and `aptos-vm-environment` now depend on this lightweight crate instead of the full `aptos-framework`.

**Result:** Changing the Move compiler no longer triggers a rebuild of `aptos-vm` or any VM-dependent crate.

### 2. `aptos-release-bundle`

Extracts `ReleaseBundle`/`ReleasePackage` types into a lightweight crate with no compiler/prover dependencies. `aptos-cached-packages` and `aptos-vm-genesis` now depend on this instead of `aptos-framework`. `aptos-package-builder` is switched to depend on `aptos-framework-natives`.

**Result:** Changing `move-prover` no longer triggers a rebuild of `aptos-cached-packages`, `vm-genesis`, `aptos-node`, CLI, or any of their ~22 downstream dependents.

Both original modules are re-exported from `aptos-framework` for backwards compatibility.

### Dead code cleanup

Removes unused test infrastructure that was only reachable through the now-dead `GenesisOptions::Testnet`/`Mainnet` paths:
- `GenesisOptions` enum (only `Head` variant was reachable)
- `testnet_release_bundle()`, `testnet.mrb`
- `generate_genesis_change_set_for_mainnet()`
- `GENESIS_CHANGE_SET_TESTNET`, `GENESIS_CHANGE_SET_MAINNET`
- `FakeExecutorImpl::from_testnet_genesis/from_mainnet_genesis`
- `MoveHarness::new_testnet/new_mainnet`

- Fixes missing `rerun-if-changed` directives in `crates/aptos/build.rs` that caused unconditional rebuilds.

items.title: [consensus] Defer randomness aggregation for non-rand blocks
items.body:
## Summary

Defers expensive cryptographic aggregation (`S::aggregate()`) for blocks that don't contain randomness-consuming transactions. The execution pipeline determines early whether a block has randomness transactions via `has_rand_txns_fut`, which the rand manager uses to skip or proceed with aggregation.

### Problem

Currently, every ordered block goes through full randomness aggregation regardless of whether any transaction in the block actually consumes on-chain randomness. `S::aggregate()` is the expensive part — this change skips it for non-rand blocks.

### Design

The pipeline's `rand_txns_check()` scans transactions for randomness annotations **before** waiting on the randomness result, producing an early `has_rand_txns_fut`. This is split from `wait_for_rand()` to avoid a deadlock where `rand_check_fut` waits for randomness aggregation, but aggregation waits for `rand_check_fut`.

The rand manager wraps `has_rand_txns_fut` in a shared future that:
1. Resolves to `bool` (does the block need randomness?)
2. Sends `AggregationResult::Skip` via `decision_tx` if the answer is `false`

This shared future is cloned to two consumers:
- **RandStore aggregation task** — awaits the future before calling `S::aggregate()`. If `false`, returns early (no crypto work).
- **RandManager event loop** — receives `Skip` from `decision_rx` and sets `Randomness::default()` to unblock the block.
```

items.title: [storage] Refactor StateKvDb to follow StateMerkleDb pattern
items.body:
Refactor `StateKvDb` so that hot and cold instances are separate, matching
the pattern already established by `StateMerkleDb`. Each `StateKvDb` instance
is now parameterized by `is_hot` and `delete_on_restart`, and `open_dbs()`
returns both as distinct values in its result tuple.

This also removes the dedicated hot state KV column families
(`gen_hot_state_kv_shard_cfds`) and the embedded `hot_state_kv_db_shards`
field — hot and cold now share the same schema and differ only in DB paths.
The hot state KV schema is still subject to change (e.g. to support
reconstructing hot state upon restart); reusing the cold schema is safe for
now because `delete_on_restart` defaults to `true`, so the hot DB is wiped
on every startup.

The `hot_state_kv_db` field is currently unused but will be wired up for
hot state KV persistence in subsequent commits.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> 
> `AptosDB::open_dbs()` now opens/returns an additional `Option<StateKvDb>` for hot state, `AptosDB`/`StateStore` plumb this handle through construction and db-debugger utilities, and checkpoints now create both hot and cold KV DB checkpoints. items.title: [storage] Update hot state deferred merge metrics more frequently
items.body:
The `hot_state_deferred_merge_old_views` and `hot_state_deferred_merge_version_lag`
gauges were only updated at the end of each `run` iteration, right after publishing
a new commit. At that point `try_merge` typically fails because readers still hold
the previous view, so the metrics always showed `old_views=1` and a non-zero version
lag (equal to the batch size), even though the merge succeeds a few milliseconds
later in `next_to_commit`'s timeout retry loop.

- Extract `update_deferred_merge_gauges()` helper to avoid duplication.
- Call it in `try_merge` on both the success path (lag=0 after merge) and the
  `return false` path (accurate lag while blocked by lingering readers).
- Move `hot_state_items` / `hot_state_key_bytes` / `hot_state_value_bytes` gauges
  into `apply_delta_to_base`, since that's the only place that mutates DashMaps
  and byte counters.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> **Low Risk**
> Metrics-only refactor with minor control-flow changes around when gauges are updated; no changes to merge logic or persisted data paths.

items.title: [Storage] Reduce RocksDB property reporter overhead
items.body:
Each invocation of the property reporter takes 10–15 seconds in production
(sometimes nearly 20), causing the reporter thread to consume roughly half a
core just for metrics collection.

Remove 13 low-value properties from `RocksdbPropertyReporter` and increase the
reporting interval from 10s to 60s.

The most impactful removal is `rocksdb.block-cache-pinned-usage`, which performs
a full O(cache_size) scan of the shared HyperClockCache **while holding the
per-DB mutex**, directly blocking concurrent writes. The remaining removed
properties (`num-entries-*`, `num-deletes-*`, `estimate-num-keys`,
`num-snapshots`, `num-live-versions`, etc.) still acquire the per-DB mutex on
each call but are rarely consulted and provide little actionable benefit.

- Properties queried per cycle: 32 → 19 (~41% fewer)
- Sleep between cycles: 10s → 60s

For reference, removing the property reporter entirely and running the executor
benchmark (20 baseline vs 20 test runs, 10M txns each) showed +3.3% overall
TPS and +13.2% commit TPS (p < 0.001, Cohen's d = 1.64). This commit should
capture most of that improvement.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Risk**
> Metrics-only change that reduces sampling frequency and queried properties; main risk is losing visibility into the removed RocksDB metrics.
> 
> **Overview**
> Reduces `RocksdbPropertyReporter` overhead by removing a set of RocksDB properties from `ROCKSDB_PROPERTY_MAP` (including `rocksdb.block-cache-pinned-usage` and several entry/delete/snapshot-related stats), lowering the number of per-CF/per-shard `get_property` calls made each cycle.
> 
> Increases the production reporting interval from 10s to 60s (tests remain effectively unchanged), reducing mutex contention and background CPU spent on metrics collection.

items.title: [execution] Parallelize sequence number resync in executor benchmark
items.body:
`resync_sequence_numbers` reads each account's sequence number from the
DB sequentially. On a large DB, hundreds of thousands of RocksDB lookups
become I/O-bound and take minutes on a single thread.

Use `par_iter_mut()` (same pattern as the existing
`verify_sequence_numbers`) to read all accounts in parallel across
rayon's thread pool.

> **Overview**

items.title: [Execution] Fix seed account balance exhaustion in executor benchmark
items.body:
The seed account balance was hardcoded as `init_account_balance * 10_000`,
which doesn't scale when the number of accounts per seed account exceeds
10,000 (e.g. creating 1 billion accounts with 100K seed accounts). This
caused `EINSUFFICIENT_BALANCE` aborts as seed accounts ran out of funds.

Compute seed account balance dynamically based on the actual expected
number of accounts per seed, with a large margin for gas fees and random
distribution variance.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Risk**
> Small, localized change to benchmark-only account-funding math; main risk is potential under/over-funding due to arithmetic edge cases for extreme inputs.
> 
> **Overview**
> Fixes executor-benchmark minting failures by replacing the hardcoded seed funding assumption ("enough for 10k accounts") with a dynamic calculation based on `num_new_accounts` and the derived `num_seed_accounts`.
> 
> `run_mint` now computes both the seed account count and per-seed balance (with a 50% buffer), and `create_seed_accounts` is updated to accept `num_seed_accounts` directly rather than recomputing it, preventing `EINSUFFICIENT_BALANCE` when scaling to very large account creation runs.
>

items.title: [consensus] Fix WVUF batch verification off-by-one and log errors
items.body:
## Description

Two fixes for WVUF batch verification in the randomness pipeline:

1. **Off-by-one in proof length check** (`crates/aptos-dkg/src/weighted_vuf/pinkas/mod.rs`): `verify_proof` rejected valid proofs when `proof.len() == apks.len()`, because the guard used `>=` instead of `>`. This caused batch verification to fail when a validator happens to collect shares from all validators, forcing an unnecessary fallback to individual share verification.

2. **Silent error in `pre_aggregate_verify`** (`consensus/src/rand/rand_gen/types.rs`): When batch verification failed, the error from `WVUF::verify_proof` was silently dropped via `.is_ok()`. Replaced with a `match` that logs the actual error in the existing `warn!` message, making verification failures diagnosable.

## How Has This Been Tested?

- `cargo test -p aptos-consensus -- test_pre_aggregate_verify_happy_path`
- `cargo test -p aptos-consensus -- test_pre_aggregate_verify_with_bad_share`
- `cargo check -p aptos-consensus`

## Key Areas to Review

- `crates/aptos-dkg/src/weighted_vuf/pinkas/mod.rs:219`: The bounds check change from `>=` to `>`. Having `proof.len() == apks.len()` is valid when all validators contribute shares.
- `consensus/src/rand/rand_gen/types.rs:154-177`: The `.is_ok()` pattern replaced with `match` to log the error on the `Err` branch.

This ensures the SDK works as expected in these diverse JavaScript runtimes. ### Test Plan The new GitHub Actions workflows (`.github/workflows/run-bun-tests.yaml`, `.github/workflows/run-deno-tests.yaml`, and `.github/workflows/run-web-tests.yaml`) will automatically execute on every pull request and push to `main`. These workflows set up a local testnet and run basic SDK functionality tests (e.g., account creation, funding, transfers, sponsored transactions) within their respective environments. ### Related Links ### Checklist - [ ] Have you ran `pnpm fmt`? - [x] Have you updated the `CHANGELOG.md`? --- [Slack Thread](https://aptos-org.slack.com/archives/C05NLAKJM9U/p1770126464644589?thread_ts=1770126464.644589&cid=C05NLAKJM9U)

items.title: fix(examples): make multisig_v2 type-safe under strict TypeScript checks
items.body:
### Description

 - This PR fixes strict TypeScript errors in examples/typescript-esm/multisig_v2.ts.
  - Errors addressed
      - TS2538: Type 'undefined' cannot be used as an index type (network env indexing)
      - TS18048: '...transaction_payload' is possibly 'undefined' (multiple multisig payload
        byte accesses)
  - What changed
      - Added a safe env parsing pattern before indexing NetworkToNetworkName.
      - Added getMultisigTransactionPayloadBytes(...) to enforce runtime/compile-time safety
        for optional transaction_payload.
      - Replaced all direct multiSig.transaction_payload.bcsToBytes() usages with the
        helper.
  - Result
      - pnpm --dir examples/typescript-esm build passes for the multisig_v2 example with
        strict checks enabled.

items.title: Dependencies security vulnerabilities
items.body: ### Description This PR addresses and resolves all identified security vulnerabilities in the dependencies of the `examples/` and `projects/` subdirectories. Key changes include: * Replacing `npm-run-all` with `npm-run-all2` in all examples to mitigate `minimatch` ReDoS vulnerabilities. * Updating `superagent` in `examples/typescript` to fix `qs` DoS vulnerabilities. * Updating `tsup` and adding `pnpm.overrides` for `diff` and `glob` in `examples/typescript-esm` to resolve `glob` command injection and `diff` DoS vulnerabilities. All sub-projects now pass `pnpm audit` with no known vulnerabilities. ### Test Plan 1. Run `pnpm install` in the root directory to regenerate lockfiles. 2. Navigate to each `examples/` subdirectory (`javascript`, `typescript`, `typescript-esm`) and `projects/gas-station`. 3. Run `pnpm audit` in each of these directories and confirm that no vulnerabilities are reported. ### Related Links ### Checklist - [x] Have you ran `pnpm fmt`? - [x] Have you updated the `CHANGELOG.md`? ---

items.title: Gas estimation regression
items.body: ### Description This PR addresses a gas estimation regression (issue #827) with a two-part fix: 1. **Simulation Retry**: When `simulateTransaction` is called with `estimateMaxGasAmount: true` and the server returns `MAX_GAS_UNITS_BELOW_MIN_TRANSACTION_GAS_UNITS` (due to an unreasonably low gas estimate), the SDK now automatically retries the simulation without the `estimateMaxGasAmount` flag. This ensures the transaction's original, reasonable `max_gas_amount` (default 200,000) is used. 2. **Minimum Gas Floor**: A new `MIN_MAX_GAS_AMOUNT = 2000` constant is introduced and enforced in `generateRawTransaction`. If a user explicitly provides a `maxGasAmount` below this minimum, it is clamped up to 2000, preventing transactions from being built with gas amounts that would be immediately rejected by the network. ### Test Plan - Verify that simulating a transaction with `estimateMaxGasAmount: true` that would otherwise fail with `MAX_GAS_UNITS_BELOW_MIN_TRANSACTION_GAS_UNITS` now succeeds. - Confirm that providing a `maxGasAmount` below 2000 when generating a raw transaction results in the `max_gas_amount` being set to 2000. - Existing unit tests cover the minimum gas amount enforcement. - Ran `pnpm lint`, `pnpm build`. ### Related Links - Fixes #827 ### Checklist - [x] Have you ran `pnpm fmt`? - [x] Have you updated the `CHANGELOG.md`? --- [Slack Thread](https://aptos-org.slack.com/archives/C05NLAKJM9U/p1771534137734559?thread_ts=1771534137.734559&cid=C05NLAKJM9U)

items.body: Bumps `aptos-sdk` to v0.4.0 and `aptos-sdk-macros` to v0.2.0, updating changelogs to reflect security fixes, dependency upgrades, and breaking changes for release. The `aptos-sdk` v0.4.0 release includes comprehensive security audit remediations, ECDSA signature hardening, and migration from `hex` to `const-hex`. `aptos-sdk-macros` v0.2.0 introduces breaking changes in generated code to align with `const-hex` and adds security hardening for path traversal and identifier validation. ---

items.title: Various performance and security fixes
items.body:
## Summary

Comprehensive security hardening and performance improvements based on a 3-pass security audit of the SDK. The audit report is included as `SECURITY_AUDIT.md`.

### Security hardening

- **Streaming response bounds**: All HTTP response reads now use `read_response_bounded()` which pre-checks `Content-Length` and reads incrementally via chunked streaming, aborting early if the size limit is exceeded. This prevents OOM from malicious servers using chunked transfer-encoding. Error body reads are also bounded.
- **Low-S ECDSA enforcement**: Both secp256k1 and secp256r1 now enforce low-S signatures in signing, parsing (`from_bytes`/`from_hex`), and verification to match aptos-core's on-chain behavior and prevent signature malleability.
- **Codegen injection prevention**: ABI-derived strings are sanitized via `sanitize_abi_string()` before embedding in generated Rust code. Module names are validated against path traversal and Rust keywords. `format_ident!` calls are guarded with `safe_format_ident()` using raw identifiers for keywords.
- **Zeroization**: Entropy, seed, and intermediate key material in mnemonic derivation are explicitly zeroized. `Pepper` type uses `ZeroizeOnDrop` with a redacted `Debug` impl. Ed25519 private key buffer is zeroized after construction.
- **URL scheme validation**: All API client constructors (`with_url`) validate URL schemes to prevent SSRF via `file://`, `gopher://`, etc.
- **Error sanitization**: `sanitized_message()` redacts sensitive patterns (private keys, mnemonics, JWTs, API keys) and URL query parameters. Error body truncation prevents log flooding. UTF-8 boundary-safe truncation.
- **Proc macro safety**: `aptos_contract_file!` canonicalizes paths and verifies they remain under `CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR` to prevent path traversal at compile time.

### Performance improvements

- **AtomicU8 for chain ID**: Replaced `RwLock<u8>` with `AtomicU8` for chain ID caching, eliminating lock contention on every transaction build.

items.title: Replace hex crate with const-hex
items.body:
## Summary
- Replace `hex` crate with `const-hex` for hex encoding/decoding (faster, `const`-compatible)
- Add `serde` feature to `const-hex` for serde deserialization helpers
- Re-export `const_hex` from `aptos-sdk` so generated code (codegen + proc macros) works without downstream crates needing a direct `const-hex` dependency
- Fix clippy `needless_borrows_for_generic_args` warnings

Supersedes #35

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo test -p aptos-sdk --all-features` — all 875 unit tests + 31 doctests pass
- [x] `cargo clippy -p aptos-sdk --all-features -- -D warnings` — zero warnings
- [x] `cargo fmt -- --check` — clean

items.title: Potential fix for code scanning alert no. 21: Workflow does not contain permissions
items.body:
Potential fix for [https://github.com/aptos-labs/aptos-wallet-adapter/security/code-scanning/21](https://github.com/aptos-labs/aptos-wallet-adapter/security/code-scanning/21)

In general, fix this kind of issue by adding an explicit `permissions` block either at the workflow root (applies to all jobs) or at the job level, granting only the minimal scopes required (often `contents: read` and optionally `packages: read`). This documents the intended permissions and prevents accidental escalation if repository defaults change.

For this specific workflow in `.github/workflows/build.yml`, the `build` job only checks out the code, sets up Node and pnpm, installs dependencies, and runs a build. None of these steps require write access to the repository or to pull requests. A safe, minimal configuration is to set `permissions: contents: read` at the workflow root, so it applies to all jobs (currently only `build`). To implement this, insert:

```yaml
permissions:
  contents: read
```

after the `on:` block (after line 5 in the provided snippet) and before the `concurrency:` block. No additional methods, imports, or other definitions are necessary.

items.title: feat: Add Watchee wallet to AIP-62 registry
items.body:
## Summary

- Adds [Watchee](https://watcheetv.com) to the AIP-62 wallet registry with brand icon and `watcheetv://` deep link provider
- Adds `"Watchee"` to the `AvailableWallets` type union
- Adds Watchee to the supported wallets list in README

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `registry.ts` | Add Watchee entry with icon, URL, and `deeplinkProvider` |
| `types.ts` | Add `"Watchee"` to `AvailableWallets` |
| `README.md` | Add Watchee to supported wallets list |

## Design Decision: Registry-only (no SDK wallet)

Watchee is a **mobile wallet** (React Native app). Unlike browser extension wallets, it should NOT be registered as an SDK wallet in `sdkWallets.ts` because:

1. SDK wallets are unconditionally marked as `Installed`, which prevents the registry's `deeplinkProvider` from being used
2. On mobile, clicking Connect should redirect to the Watchee app via `watcheetv://wallet/browse?url=` deep link
3. Inside the Watchee WebView (mini-apps), the app's native bridge handles wallet registration automatically

This follows the same pattern as Petra and Nightly, which use registry + deep link without SDK wallet registration.

## About Watchee

Watchee is a streaming platform with a mini-app ecosystem built on Aptos. The [`@watchee/wallet-adapter-plugin`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@watchee/wallet-adapter-plugin) npm package provides the AIP-62 compatible wallet interface for dApps running inside Watchee's WebView.

## Test Plan

- [ ] Watchee appears in the wallet selector UI (under "More Wallets" when not detected)
- [ ] Deep link redirects to Watchee app on mobile (`watcheetv://wallet/browse?url=<dapp-url>`)
- [ ] Existing wallet flows unaffected (no changes to `sdkWallets.ts` or `package.json`)
- [ ] TypeScript compiles without errors

items.body: ### Description This PR resolves three high-severity security vulnerabilities: 1. **Rollup Arbitrary File Write (GHSA-mw96-cpmx-2vgc)**: Upgraded `rollup` from `4.57.1` to `4.59.0` to mitigate a path traversal vulnerability. 2. **Minimatch ReDoS (GHSA-7r86-cg39-jmmj, GHSA-23c5-xmqv-rm74)**: Updated pnpm overrides for `minimatch` from `>=10.2.1` to `>=10.2.3` (resolved to `10.2.4`) to fix two regular expression denial-of-service vulnerabilities. All changes pass formatting, linting, and build checks. ### Related Links * #125 (Rollup Arbitrary File Write via Path Traversal) * #126 (minimatch ReDoS: nested *() extglobs) * #127 (minimatch ReDoS: matchOne() combinatorial backtracking) ### Checklist * [x] Code changes implemented. * [x] Dependencies updated. * [x] Formatting, linting, and build pass. ---

items.title: Robots.txt LLM validation
items.body: ### Description Comments out invalid `LLMs:` and `LLMs-full:` directives in `robots.txt`. These lines are not part of the `robots.txt` specification and cause validation errors with tools like Google's validator. The `llms.txt` standard relies on well-known paths for discovery, not `robots.txt` directives. The lines are retained as comments for documentation. ### Related Links ### Checklist ---

items.title: Eslint biome migration
items.body: ### Description Migrates the project from ESLint and Prettier to Biome for linting and formatting. **Motivation:** - Consolidate linting and formatting into a single, high-performance tool. - Streamline development workflow and dependency management. **Key Changes:** - Replaced ESLint and Prettier configurations (`eslint.config.js`, `.prettierrc.json`, `.prettierignore`) and their dev dependencies with Biome. - Introduced `biome.json` configured to closely match the previous ESLint + Prettier setup. - Updated `package.json` scripts (`lint`, `fmt`, `check`) and `lint-staged` to utilize Biome commands. - Converted existing `eslint-disable` comments to `biome-ignore` / `biome-ignore-all` equivalents. - Applied Biome's auto-fixes for formatting, import organization, and common linting issues across the codebase. - Configured new Biome rules (not previously enforced by ESLint) to `warn` level for gradual adoption. - Updated `AGENTS.md` to reflect the new tooling. All `pnpm lint`, `pnpm fmt`, and `pnpm check` commands now pass successfully. ### Related Links ### Checklist ---

items.title: feat(seo): aria-labels, SSR head metadata, hreflang, and LLM discoverability
items.body:
## Summary

- **Semantic table labels**: Added `aria-label` and `data-entity-type` attributes to all 16 data tables across the app. This helps screen readers identify table contents and gives AI parsers a semantic hint about the entity type without parsing cell data.
- **SSR `head()` on landing page**: Moved `index.tsx` metadata from client-side `<PageMetadata>` (Helmet) to TanStack Router's SSR `head()`, so crawlers that don't execute JavaScript can see title, description, and OG tags.
- **`hreflang` + `og:locale`**: Added self-referential `hreflang="en"` and `hreflang="x-default"` link tags plus `og:locale` meta to the root `head()` to signal language/locale to search engines.
- **LLM discoverability**: Added `llms.txt`, `llms-full.txt`, `opensearch.xml`, enhanced `robots.txt` with AI crawler rules, expanded `sitemap.xml`, and Netlify caching headers for LLM documentation endpoints.
- **JSON-LD structured data**: Enhanced `PageMetadata` component with BreadcrumbList, WebSite, WebPage, and page-type-specific schemas.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm fmt` — clean
- [x] `pnpm lint` — 0 errors, 0 warnings
- [x] `pnpm build` — production build succeeds
- [ ] Verify no `<Table>` elements remain without `aria-label` in `app/pages/`
- [ ] Verify SSR output for landing page includes meta tags in initial HTML
- [ ] Verify hreflang links appear in root `<head>` output
- [ ] Verify `/llms.txt` and `/llms-full.txt` are accessible and cached

items.title: Dependency security issues
items.body: ### Description This PR resolves 8 security vulnerabilities (6 high, 2 moderate) found in transitive dependencies. The fix involves: - Applying `pnpm overrides` to patch `axios`, `tar`, and `ajv`. - Migrating the ESLint configuration from `@eslint/eslintrc` (FlatCompat) to native flat config APIs. This allowed for the removal of the `@eslint/eslintrc` dependency, which was the source of a `minimatch` vulnerability that could not be resolved via overrides. All changes have been verified: - `pnpm audit` reports 0 vulnerabilities. - `pnpm lint` passes cleanly. - `pnpm test` passes all tests. ### Related Links ### Checklist ---
